problem_addressed: Existing unconventional computing approaches require problem transformations (embedding, sparsification, quadratisation) that degrade solution quality for higher-order, densely connected, or reconfigurable combinatorial optimization problems.
core_claim: A virtually connected probabilistic computer (VCPC) using photonic quantum random number generators outperforms digital annealing units by orders of magnitude on Erdős–Rényi graph spin-glasses without requiring problem transformations.
key_contributions: Demonstration of VCPC's capability to solve higher-order problems (hypergraph hitting set) directly without quadratisation
Implementation of greedy graph coloring for hardware parallelisation enabling favourable scaling
Quantitative comparison showing quadratisation/sparsification would increase problem size by 10-25x for k=5/10 hypergraphs
novelty: Virtual hardware connections circumvent topological constraints of physical hardware, eliminating necessity for problem transformations that deteriorate solution quality in existing approaches.
method_summary: Uses high-speed photonic quantum random number generators to create a probabilistic computing architecture with virtual connections between p-bits. Solves higher-order problems via simulated annealing (PC-SA) with greedy graph coloring for parallelization.
method_components: Photonic quantum random number generators for probabilistic p-bit states
Virtual connectivity layer enabling arbitrary problem topologies
Greedy hypergraph coloring for parallel p-bit updates
Energy function formulation for hypergraph hitting set (Eqn. 13)
key_innovation: Virtual hardware connections allowing direct mapping of arbitrary combinatorial optimization problems to hardware without transformation, preserving problem structure and solution quality.
assumptions: Simulations accurately model VCPC hardware behavior
Photonic RNGs can generate high-speed random numbers
Greedy coloring provides sufficient parallelization for desired scaling
theoretical_grounding: Derivation of p-bit update drive for hitting set problem (Eqn. 14), scaling analysis O(m(k-1)), and performance comparison against Lovász's (1+ln d) approximation bound for set cover.
hyperparameters: Clock speed: 2.7 GHz; Solution quality thresholds: q=0.5, 0.8; Graph density p: 0.1-1.0; Sparsification ratio rs: up to 50
headline_result: TTS of ~10⁻⁵ seconds for q=0.8 and N=1024, outperforming DA by orders of magnitude
ablations: KMC masking vs. unmasked p-bit arrays
Sparsification ratio rs impact on rN
improvement_over_baseline: TTS reduced by ~100× compared to digital annealing for q=0.8, N=1024
best_result: TTS of ~10⁻⁵ seconds for q=0.8 and N=1024, outperforming DA by orders of magnitude
title: A virtually connected probabilistic computer as a solver for higher-order, densely connected, or reconfigurable combinatorial optimisation problems
